    "GDP growth remains robust at 7.8% (Q3 FY26) with RBI projecting FY26 full-year at 7.6%; domestic economic cycle is in late-expansion phase (capacity utilisation 75.6%, above 75% threshold), providing a constructive underlying backdrop for equity-oriented allocations.",
    "RBI repo rate at 5.25% following 125 bps of cuts from 6.50%; cycle is in early-pause phase with neutral stance maintained unanimously at April 8, 2026 MPC meeting; forward path is hold through near-term given West Asia uncertainty.",
    "Real interest rate derived at approximately 3.15% (repo 5.25% minus core CPI 2.1%), which is materially above the +2% restrictive threshold; this is the highest real rate in several years and represents a meaningful opportunity cost for equity risk-taking at the margin.",
    "CPI headline at 3.40% (March 2026), below RBI's 4% target; core CPI at 2.1% (Jan-Feb average); inflation is currently benign, but IMD forecasts below-normal monsoon at 92% of LPA with 66% probability of below-normal or deficient outcome — a material upside inflation risk for H2 FY27 that could constrain further RBI easing.",
    "Brent crude at $118.95/barrel (April 29, 2026), up approximately 86% year-on-year from ~$64; Hormuz disruption blocking approximately 20% of global oil trade is the proximate driver; India as a net crude importer faces direct pass-through pressure on current account, fiscal subsidy burden, and input cost inflation.",
    "INR at 94.79 vs USD (April 29, 2026), representing 10.7% depreciation over 12 months from 85.60; forex reserves have declined $25 billion to $703 billion; currency stress is active and not yet stabilised.",
    "FII net equity outflows of Rs 56,401 Cr in April 2026 alone (~$5.97 billion), with the heaviest single-day outflow of Rs 19,837 Cr on April 2; DII absorption of Rs 34,617 Cr (partial April 1-8 data) is not fully offsetting; the FII-DII dynamic is stressed.",
    "India-US rate differential at 162 bps (5.25% minus 3.625%) sits within the adequate 100-200 bps range but is not wide enough to attract aggressive carry-driven FII inflows; with Fed on indefinite hold and leadership transition (Warsh replacing Powell ~May 15), US policy uncertainty adds an external variable.",
    "10Y government bond yield at 6.99% (April 17, 2026), up 44 bps in five months from 6.55% in November 2025, with an intraday spike to 7.19% on April 3; rising long-end yields reflect crude-driven inflation risk premium and are a headwind to equity valuation multiples via discount rate expansion.",
    "PMI Manufacturing rebounded to 55.9 (April flash) after a March dip to 53.9 attributed to Middle East conflict; PMI Services at 57.9 (April flash); Composite PMI at 58.3 — domestic activity indicators remain above long-run averages, providing a floor to earnings expectations.",
    "Government fiscal consolidation is on track: fiscal deficit YTD at 80.38% of RE with a 7.0% YoY reduction; capex at 84.81% of RE, up 14.46% YoY; FY27 budget targets fiscal deficit at 4.3% of GDP with capex of Rs 12.22 lakh crore — quality of expenditure improving, supportive of infrastructure and manufacturing earnings.",
    "SEBI PMS regulatory framework: SEBI (Portfolio Managers) Regulations 2020 overhaul (effective January 16, 2020) and SEBI minimum ticket of Rs 50 lakh (sebi_001) are both satisfied by the proposed Rs 1 Cr ticket; no pending regulatory inflection specific to PMS category within the decision window.",
    "SIF (Specialised Investment Fund) framework effective April 1, 2025 introduces a new product tier between PMS and AIF; this is a structural regulatory development in the managed-account space but does not directly constrain or alter the PMS product being evaluated.",
    "Non-food credit growth at 14.3% YoY (February 2026), rising from 11.1% a year prior; credit expansion is supportive of corporate earnings but also signals that monetary transmission, while occurring (WALR declining from 9.37% peak to 8.28%), remains incomplete."

  "reasoning_paragraph": "The macro environment as of April 2026 presents a bifurcated picture: domestic fundamentals remain constructive — GDP growth at 7.8% (Q3 FY26), PMI Composite at 58.3, fiscal consolidation on track with capex up 14.46% YoY, and CPI headline at 3.40% below the RBI's 4% target — but the external risk overlay has deteriorated sharply and is the dominant near-term signal. Brent crude at $118.95/barrel (up approximately 86% year-on-year) driven by a Hormuz disruption affecting approximately 20% of global oil trade is transmitting simultaneously into INR weakness (94.79, down 10.7% over 12 months), rising long-end yields (10Y at 6.99%, up 44 bps in five months with an April 3 spike to 7.19%), and heavy FII equity outflows (Rs 56,401 Cr in April alone). The real interest rate, derived at approximately 3.15% (repo 5.25% minus core CPI 2.1%), is materially above the restrictive threshold and represents a genuine opportunity cost for incremental equity risk. The RBI's April 8 unanimous hold with a neutral stance and explicit citation of West Asia uncertainty signals that the central bank is in a watchful posture rather than an easing one, and the IMD's below-normal monsoon forecast (92% of LPA, 66% probability of below-normal or deficient outcome) introduces a credible H2 FY27 food inflation risk that could further constrain the easing path. Against this backdrop, the proposed action — a Rs 1 Cr first-time PMS allocation funded partly by a mutual fund trim — is being evaluated in a window of active external stress rather than a settled macro environment; the PMS product itself is regulatory-compliant (SEBI minimum ticket satisfied, 2020 framework in force, no pending regulatory inflection), and the domestic growth and earnings cycle does not contradict the long-term equity thesis, but the timing coincides with a period of elevated currency, crude, and capital-flow volatility that is material context for a new, less-liquid product entry.",
